payload.name: Big O
payload.description:
Write a report that explains and applies Big O notation to algorithm analysis. Focus on showing how asymptotic growth describes performance as input size increases.

  1. Define Big O notation in your own words and explain why it is useful for comparing algorithms.
  2. Analyze at least three algorithms or operations from the course and describe their time complexity in best-case, average-case, or worst-case terms as appropriate.
  3. Compare two algorithms that solve the same problem and explain how their growth rates differ as input size increases.
  4. Use at least one concrete example or small input trace to illustrate how you determined the complexity of a loop, recursive process, or data-structure operation.
  5. Conclude by discussing what Big O does and does not tell us about real-world performance.

Deliverables:

  • A written report with a clear introduction, body, and conclusion.
  • Your complexity analyses and any examples used to support them.

rubric_criteria.label: Correctness
rubric_criteria.description: Complexity analyses are accurate, definitions are correct, and Big O notation is applied appropriately.

rubric_criteria.label: Analysis depth
rubric_criteria.description: Explains comparisons clearly, includes meaningful examples, and demonstrates solid reasoning about growth rates.

rubric_criteria.label: Clarity
rubric_criteria.description: Report is well-organized, readable, and presents ideas in a focused, professional way.

reason: This will permanently delete the assignment and all associated student submissions and grades. This cannot be undone.

payload.name: Recursion Practice in C++
payload.description:
Implement the following recursive functions in C++.

  1. factorial(n): compute n! recursively for n >= 0.
  2. fibonacci(n): compute the nth Fibonacci number recursively, with fibonacci(0) == 0 and fibonacci(1) == 1.
  3. sum_to_n(n): recursively compute the sum of all integers from 1 to n for n >= 1.

Deliverables:

  • Submit your completed C++ source files.
  • Ensure each function is implemented recursively and handles the stated edge cases.

rubric_criteria.label: Correctness
rubric_criteria.description: Each recursive function returns the correct result for valid inputs, including the required base cases.

rubric_criteria.label: Recursive design
rubric_criteria.description: Solutions use recursion appropriately and do not rely on iterative shortcuts for the required functions.

rubric_criteria.label: Code quality
rubric_criteria.description: Code is organized, readable, and follows standard C++ conventions.

payload.name: Sorting
payload.description:
In this assignment, you will implement and compare two sorting algorithms in Java, with attention to correctness, efficiency, and design choices.

  1. Implement the first sorting routine so that it correctly sorts an array of integers in ascending order.
  2. Implement the second sorting routine so that it also sorts an array of integers in ascending order, using a different approach from the first routine.
  3. Test both routines on multiple input sizes and briefly describe any differences you observe in performance or behavior.
  4. Reflect on the trade-offs between the two approaches, including time complexity, space usage, and readability.

Deliverables:

  • Your completed Java source file(s).
  • A short write-up summarizing your test results and comparison.

rubric_criteria.label: Correctness
rubric_criteria.description: Both sorting implementations produce correct ascending order results across the required test cases.

rubric_criteria.label: Correctness
rubric_criteria.description: The second sorting implementation is correct, follows the required approach, and handles edge cases properly.

payload.name: Semaphores
payload.description:
Implement and analyze a semaphore-based synchronization solution in C for a concurrent systems problem. Your goal is to demonstrate that you can design correct coordination between multiple threads/processes and explain the synchronization choices you made.

  1. Implement a semaphore abstraction and use it to coordinate access to a shared resource in a multi-producer/multi-consumer setting.
  2. Show how your solution prevents race conditions, deadlock, and lost wakeups under the stated constraints.
  3. Analyze the correctness of your synchronization strategy, including why each semaphore operation is placed where it is.
  4. Discuss the performance implications of your design and any trade-offs in fairness, throughput, and simplicity.

Deliverables:

  • Your C source file(s) with complete, compilable implementations.
  • A short written analysis explaining the synchronization design and correctness arguments.

rubric_criteria.label: Correctness
rubric_criteria.description: The semaphore-based implementation compiles and correctly synchronizes access to shared state without race conditions or deadlock.

rubric_criteria.label: Analysis depth
rubric_criteria.description: The written and/or inline explanation shows a deep understanding of why the synchronization works and discusses trade-offs clearly.

rubric_criteria.label: Clarity
rubric_criteria.description: Code, comments, and analysis are organized, readable, and precise.
